subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in Text 1?
Text 1
In 2019 Bergamo, Italy, was named a City of Gastronomy by UNESCO in recognition of the city's unique food culture. <u>The honor is well known among both residents of the city and tourists who visit</u>. It's obvious that the selection of Bergamo by UNESCO has brought awareness to local recipes, cooking practices, and chefs and has provided a boost to the city's tourism industry.
Text 2
Many residents of Bergamo hoped that food tourism would increase after the city was chosen as a City of Gastronomy in 2019. However, as researcher Minji Cho and colleagues argue, cities must still create effective marketing strategies to benefit fully from being named a City of Gastronomy. Without an intentional effort to promote the city's food scene, many current and potential visitors to Bergamo may not even be aware that it's home to uniquely delicious food.
passage:
Text 1
In 2019 Bergamo, Italy, was named a City of Gastronomy by UNESCO in recognition of the city's unique food culture. <u>The honor is well known among both residents of the city and tourists who visit</u>. It's obvious that the selection of Bergamo by UNESCO has brought awareness to local recipes, cooking practices, and chefs and has provided a boost to the city's tourism industry.
Text 2
Many residents of Bergamo hoped that food tourism would increase after the city was chosen as a City of Gastronomy in 2019. However, as researcher Minji Cho and colleagues argue, cities must still create effective marketing strategies to benefit fully from being named a City of Gastronomy. Without an intentional effort to promote the city's food scene, many current and potential visitors to Bergamo may not even be aware that it's home to uniquely delicious food.
questionText: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in Text 1?

choices.text: By arguing that UNESCO's recognition of Bergamo was well deserved because of the city's delicious food.
By suggesting that Bergamo's status as a City of Gastronomy may not be well known outside of Italy.
By noting that there are other benefits to being named a City of Gastronomy besides increased tourism.
By claiming that Bergamo's chefs were mainly responsible for the city's recognition by UNESCO.

subject: reading
prompt:
According to the text, what is true about the aria "I Got Plenty o' Nuttin'"?
Known for the albums *Quiet Nights* and *Milestones*, jazz trumpeter Miles Davis collaborated several times with pianist Gil Evans. Their 1958 adaptation of George Gershwin's opera *Porgy and Bess* bears little resemblance to the 1935 original. Davis and Evans felt no desire to please listeners expecting an exact duplication of the opera. They omitted parts, such as the aria "I Got Plenty o' Nuttin'," and sometimes made only brief gestures toward Gershwin's melodies. But Davis and Evans's willingness to recompose Gershwin's work led to one of the most enduring albums in Davis's catalog.
passage: Known for the albums *Quiet Nights* and *Milestones*, jazz trumpeter Miles Davis collaborated several times with pianist Gil Evans. Their 1958 adaptation of George Gershwin's opera *Porgy and Bess* bears little resemblance to the 1935 original. Davis and Evans felt no desire to please listeners expecting an exact duplication of the opera. They omitted parts, such as the aria "I Got Plenty o' Nuttin'," and sometimes made only brief gestures toward Gershwin's melodies. But Davis and Evans's willingness to recompose Gershwin's work led to one of the most enduring albums in Davis's catalog.
questionText: According to the text, what is true about the aria "I Got Plenty o' Nuttin'"?

choices.text: It appears in the Gershwin opera *Porgy and Bess* but not in the adaptation by Davis and Evans.
A brief quotation from it appears in Davis and Evans's adaptation of *Porgy and Bess*.
Listeners familiar with Gershwin's body of work generally disliked it.
It was adapted by Davis in a recording that appears on his album *Milestones*.

subject: reading
prompt:

The following text is adapted from Daniel Defoe's 1704 nonfiction book *The Storm*.
The sermon is a sound of words spoken to the ear, and prepared only for present meditation, and extends no farther than the strength of memory can convey it; a book printed is a record, remaining in every man's possession, always ready to renew its acquaintance with his memory, and always ready to be produced as an authority or voucher to any reports he makes out of it, and conveys its contents for ages to come, to the eternity of mortal time, when the author is forgotten in his grave.
passage:
The following text is adapted from Daniel Defoe's 1704 nonfiction book *The Storm*.
The sermon is a sound of words spoken to the ear, and prepared only for present meditation, and extends no farther than the strength of memory can convey it; a book printed is a record, remaining in every man's possession, always ready to renew its acquaintance with his memory, and always ready to be produced as an authority or voucher to any reports he makes out of it, and conveys its contents for ages to come, to the eternity of mortal time, when the author is forgotten in his grave.
questionText: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Words committed to print have a greater permanence than messages that are merely spoken aloud.
People are less likely to forget a message when they hear it spoken aloud than they are when they read it in print.
Unless a spoken message is delivered by an expert, it can be safely ignored.
Most authors have little hope of being remembered well past their lifetimes.

prompt:
Which finding, if true, would most directly weaken the underlined claim?
Puerto Rico is an island in the Caribbean Sea. Indigenous people there started raising guinea pigs about 1,700 years ago. Guinea pigs had originally been domesticated much earlier in both Colombia and Peru. So were guinea pigs brought to Puerto Rico from Colombia or from Peru? Ancient Caribbean trade routes connected Puerto Rico with Colombia but not with Peru. <u>Therefore, guinea pigs in Puerto Rico probably came from Colombia and descended from Colombian guinea pigs.</u>
passage: Puerto Rico is an island in the Caribbean Sea. Indigenous people there started raising guinea pigs about 1,700 years ago. Guinea pigs had originally been domesticated much earlier in both Colombia and Peru. So were guinea pigs brought to Puerto Rico from Colombia or from Peru? Ancient Caribbean trade routes connected Puerto Rico with Colombia but not with Peru. <u>Therefore, guinea pigs in Puerto Rico probably came from Colombia and descended from Colombian guinea pigs.</u>
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly weaken the underlined claim?

choices.text: Guinea pigs are common in ancient Puerto Rican art, especially in pottery.
Ancient guinea pigs in Puerto Rico were genetically less similar to ancient guinea pigs in Colombia than to ancient guinea pigs in Peru.
The guinea pig population of ancient Colombia was much larger than the guinea pig population of ancient Peru.
Modern breeds of guinea pigs don't look like images of guinea pigs in ancient art from Puerto Rico, Colombia, and Peru.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
Unlike modern giant pandas, which are exclusive to Asia, ancient panda species were dispersed across Europe and Asia. A debate in paleontological discourse focuses on whether ancient pandas first emerged in Asia before migrating to Europe, or vice versa. A pair of European panda teeth found in Bulgaria led researchers to conclude that they likely belonged to an undiscovered species of panda that the researchers dubbed *Agriarctos nikolovi*. The teeth are relatively recent among European panda fossils, some of which date back to more than 10 million years ago, implying that ______.
passage: Unlike modern giant pandas, which are exclusive to Asia, ancient panda species were dispersed across Europe and Asia. A debate in paleontological discourse focuses on whether ancient pandas first emerged in Asia before migrating to Europe, or vice versa. A pair of European panda teeth found in Bulgaria led researchers to conclude that they likely belonged to an undiscovered species of panda that the researchers dubbed *Agriarctos nikolovi*. The teeth are relatively recent among European panda fossils, some of which date back to more than 10 million years ago, implying that ______.
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: pandas presumably originated in some part of Asia before migrating to Europe.
the *A. nikolovi* teeth suggest a later origin for pandas in Europe than was previously believed.
pandas likely originated in Europe since the new species was discovered in Bulgaria.
the *A. nikolovi* teeth are unlikely to assist in the resolution of the debate surrounding the origins of pandas.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which finding from the study, if true, would most directly support the researchers' conclusion?
Almost all works of fiction contain references to the progression of time, including the time of day when events in a story take place. In a 2020 study, Maya Shah, Daniel Park, and Elena Ortiz claim that an observable pattern in such references reflects a shift in human behavior prompted by the spread of electric lighting in the late nineteenth century. The researchers drew this conclusion from an analysis of more than 50,000 novels spanning many centuries and cultures, using software to recognize and tally both specific time references—that is, clock phrases, such as 7 a.m. or 2:30 p.m.—and implied ones, such as mentions of meals typically associated with a particular time of day.
passage: Almost all works of fiction contain references to the progression of time, including the time of day when events in a story take place. In a 2020 study, Maya Shah, Daniel Park, and Elena Ortiz claim that an observable pattern in such references reflects a shift in human behavior prompted by the spread of electric lighting in the late nineteenth century. The researchers drew this conclusion from an analysis of more than 50,000 novels spanning many centuries and cultures, using software to recognize and tally both specific time references—that is, clock phrases, such as 7 a.m. or 2:30 p.m.—and implied ones, such as mentions of meals typically associated with a particular time of day.
questionText: Which finding from the study, if true, would most directly support the researchers' conclusion?

choices.text: Novels published after the year 1800 include the clock phrase 10 a.m. less often than novels published before the year 1800 do.
Among novels published in the nineteenth century, implied time references become steadily more common than clock phrases as publication dates approach 1900.
The time references of noon (12 p.m.) and midnight (12 a.m.) are used with roughly the same frequency in the novels.
Novels published after 1880 contain significantly more references to activities occurring after 10 p.m. than do novels from earlier periods.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
As exemplified by Temiar songs about landforms and landmarks and Lakota songs about gathering mouse beans, ecological information can be transmitted in Indigenous songs, and in some instances is maintained only in this way. Kwaxsistalla Wathl’thla, a song keeper for the Kwakwaka’wakw people in Canada, collaborated with ethnobiologist Mira Halpern et al., sharing songs referencing terraced intertidal clam gardens the people implemented in the past to foster healthy development of a dietary staple. Drawing on archaeological evidence as well, Halpern et al. determined that the prevalence of the practice described in the songs corresponded with growth in clam size and abundance despite increased harvesting pressure—a finding that demonstrates that ______
passage: As exemplified by Temiar songs about landforms and landmarks and Lakota songs about gathering mouse beans, ecological information can be transmitted in Indigenous songs, and in some instances is maintained only in this way. Kwaxsistalla Wathl’thla, a song keeper for the Kwakwaka’wakw people in Canada, collaborated with ethnobiologist Mira Halpern et al., sharing songs referencing terraced intertidal clam gardens the people implemented in the past to foster healthy development of a dietary staple. Drawing on archaeological evidence as well, Halpern et al. determined that the prevalence of the practice described in the songs corresponded with growth in clam size and abundance despite increased harvesting pressure—a finding that demonstrates that ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: representation of practical applications of ecological knowledge is the defining characteristic of the music of certain Indigenous peoples.
the Kwakwaka’wakw people likely would not have detailed their creation of clam gardens in songs if their efforts had not produced significantly larger clams.
the clams harvested from intertidal terraces by Kwakwaka’wakw people in the past likely were a different species than the clams found in those areas today.
effective methods for the cultivation of sources of sustenance are among the ecological knowledge preserved in Indigenous songs.
